name: 14 Mall Street (Scarlet Letter House)
blurb:
Where Hawthorne wrote The Scarlet Letter (1849-1850) after being fired from the Custom House. The novel was written in a burst of creative fury.

You're standing at 14 Mall Street — the house where Nathaniel Hawthorne wrote The Scarlet Letter. He moved here with his family in 1847 after losing his job at the Custom House in a patronage purge, and from a cramped second-story room he wrote the novel that would make him America's first great novelist. He finished the manuscript in February 1850. The Scarlet Letter was published that March. Within a year he had left Salem forever, convinced his ho

name: Abbott Street Cemetery
blurb: None

name: Alice Parker Home Site
blurb:
Approximate site of Alice Parker's home. A fisherman's wife hanged September 22, 1692, in the last mass execution.

Look around: somewhere on this block stood the home of Alice Parker — a fisherman's wife swept up in the 1692 hysteria. Accused of witchcraft in May, she was convicted on spectral evidence and hanged on September 22, 1692, alongside seven others — one of the last group to die at Proctor's Ledge before the crisis collapsed. Her son-in-law John Westgate had been her most hostile accuser, describing strange visions of her in her husband's tavern. She protested her innocence to the e

name: Ames Memorial Hall
blurb: None

name: Andrew Safford House
blurb:
John Andrew was born in 1774 and a member of the firm, Archer and Andrew who engaged in trade.  When the business failed, he went to Russia as a commission merchant in St. Petersburg where he was very successful in trading goods between the two countries.  On his return to Salem, he continued to engage in shipping up until his death in 1829.

Work on the house began in 1818 and continued for the next eighteen months.  He purchased two lots at the time; one from Jonathan Hodges and the other from John Gardner.  He built the house during the time that many changes were made around The Common and

name: Armory Park
blurb: The building you are standing in front of was once the Salem Armory home to the Second Corp of Cadets, a branch of the Massachusetts National Guard.  The property was once owned by Colonel Francis Peabody and built in a late Federal style the site in 1820, with the later addition of a large banquet hall.  Armories across the country usually were built with a head house (which was administrative) and a drill shed for training.  After the purchase of the property by the Second Corps of Cadets in 1890, a drill shed was added in the back.  In 1908, the Peabody mansion was demolished and a new head

name: Benjamin Punchard, Shoreman
blurb: Benjamin Punchard, Shoreman stands as a dwelling with connections to several neighboring structures. Nearby also lie the historic buildings of the Capt. John Turner House and the Burying Point. The site shares its surroundings with the Old Town Hall and the Blancherd, Benjamin House. Its location places it near the Plan of the town of Salem, a cartographic record of the area. The presence of the Ward, John House nearby suggests a shared history within the early settlement of Salem, dating to the Commonwealth charter and the establishment of the United States.

name: Bewitched Sculpture – Samantha Statue
blurb:
The ‘Bewitched’ statue at 237-245 Essex Street honors Elizabeth Montgomery and the popular 1960s television show that filmed several episodes in Salem after a studio fire. Gifted to the city by TV Land in 2005 for the show’s 40th anniversary, the statue has become a beloved landmark and a popular photo opportunity for visitors.

The six-foot-tall statue of Elizabeth Montgomery as Samantha from the 1960s sitcom 'Bewitched' is a beloved landmark in Salem. The show filmed several episodes here in 1970 after a studio fire, and in 2005, TV Land gifted the city this bronze sculpture to celebrate the

name: Blue Anchor Tavern Site
blurb:
Site of the Blue Anchor Tavern near Salem's wharves. Waterfront taverns were where rumors spread and accusations gained momentum in 1692.

The Blue Anchor Tavern stood near here in 1692. Taverns were the nerve centers of colonial towns — where news was traded, deals struck, rumors spread, and accusations born. The Blue Anchor saw all of it during the witch trial year. Bridget Bishop, the first person hanged on June 10, 1692, ran her own tavern a few blocks away; her reputation as an unruly tavern-keeper was part of what made her a target. A tavern could make you rich — or it could get you kill

name: Broad Street Cemetery
blurb: This is the Broad Street Cemetery, consecrated in 1655 — nearly forty years before the Witch Trials. Many of the first English families of Salem are buried beneath your feet: Turners, Pickmans, Endicotts. Revolutionary War veterans rest here too, alongside ship captains from Salem's golden age of maritime trade. Read the weathered slate: the winged skulls and 'death's heads' carved on the oldest stones are a Puritan iconography, a reminder from a culture that took death seriously and saw it everywhere. By the early 1800s the style shifts — willows and urns, cherub faces, hopeful language about

name: Capt. Daniel and Mary Ingalls Bray - George C. Chase House
blurb: The Daniel Bray house is dwarfed by the surrounding houses, but there is good reason for it.  At that point in Salem’s history, the area was not considered as prestigious and many working class families lived in similar homes around the area.  The Bray family owned the land and Daniel Bray built a house on it in 1766, later purchasing the lot from his parents.  A shipmaster, he served on many of John Derby’s ships and later managed Derby Wharf after leaving the sea.  He died in 1798, but the Bray family had possession until 1856. The house found later incarnations as a grocery store, variety s

name: Castle Dismal (Manning House)
blurb:
Hawthorne's boyhood home, nicknamed Castle Dismal. Where the future author spent reclusive years reading and developing his literary voice.

Welcome to Castle Dismal — officially the Manning House. Built in the early 1800s and owned by the Manning family, this is where a young Nathaniel Hawthorne spent much of his childhood after his sea-captain father died at sea in Surinam in 1808. His mother moved the family here to live with her Manning relatives. Nathaniel read voraciously in the back parlor, walked the docks, and absorbed the stories that would later become his fiction. He called it Cast

name: Central Wharf
blurb:
Historic Central Wharf, located at 94 Wharf Street, played a vital role in Salem’s maritime history, dating back to the 1700s. Once a busy center for trade with the West Indies, China, and Europe, the wharf saw the arrival of goods like spices, silk, and rum. Today, it’s preserved as part of Salem Maritime National Historic Site, offering visitors a glimpse into Salem’s rich seafaring past.

You're standing on Central Wharf, the original heart of Salem's commercial waterfront. Built in the 18th century, it stretched out into the harbor so deep-draft merchant ships could tie up right at the war

name: Charlotte Forten Park
blurb: Salem's newest green space honors the legacy of Charlotte Forten, an abolitionist, women’s right’s activist, and Salem State University’s first African-American graduate. The roughly 25,000 square foot Charlotte Forten Park includes a plaza for programs and performances, a harbor-walk around the South River, swing seating facing the water, built-in percussion features, and green space. The park can connect to Downtown Salem, the Point neighborhood and Salem’s historic waterfront while paying tribute to Forten’s legacy and celebrating all that she achieved for generations to come. Charlotte For

name: Charter Street Cemetery
blurb: Take a moment here. This is the oldest burying ground in Salem — the Charter Street Cemetery, also called the Old Burying Point — and it has been accepting the city's dead since 1637. Walk the rows and read the slate: you're looking at three centuries of Salem condensed into stone. Governor Simon Bradstreet is buried here. So is Richard More, who came over on the Mayflower as a child of eight. And so is Judge John Hathorne, one of the magistrates who signed the death warrants for the accused in 1692. Hathorne never apologized. A few paces away, his great-great-grandson Nathaniel Hawthorne woul

name: Charter Street Historic District
blurb: You're entering the Charter Street Historic District — one of the oldest continuously-inhabited streets in English-speaking America. The first settlers laid it out in the 1630s, and the street has carried the same name, roughly the same path, for nearly four centuries. To your left is the Old Burying Point, the oldest cemetery in Salem. To your right, a row of Federal-era and Georgian houses. The Salem Witch Trials Memorial sits embedded in the district. Walk it slowly — every house, every stone, every crack in the pavement has at least two centuries of story under it.

name: Chestnut Street Historic District
blurb:
One of America's most beautiful streets. Grand Federal-style mansions built by sea captains in the early 1800s. Part of the McIntire Historic District.

Chestnut Street has been called one of the most beautiful streets in America, and standing here you understand why. The Federal-style mansions lining both sides were built in the early eighteen hundreds by Salem's merchant princes — sea captains whose ships brought back fortunes from Canton, Calcutta, and the West Indies. Most were designed by Samuel McIntire, a self-taught woodcarver turned architect whose ornamental genius is still the city'

name: Colonel Higginson
blurb:
The land on which the hotel sits was occupied since at least 1675, with Colonel Higginson constructing a large house.  In 1809 the lot came into the hands of Colonel Archer who employed Samuel McIntire to build a brick structure used for offices and stores becoming known as the Archer Block.  Captain Thomas Perkins came into possession in 1818 and renamed it the Franklin Building.  Upon his death in 1833, his estate gives over the property to the Salem Marine Society of which Captain Perkins was a member.  
The Salem Marine Society used it as their headquarters until agreeing to sell the lot w

name: Colonial Hall
blurb: Weddings and events are offered at Colonial Hall year-round and range in size from 40-300 guests. Each event is customized and includes catering, service, and equipment. Colonial Hall is located above Rockafellas restaurant inside of the historic Daniel Low and Co. Building. It was formerly a church, a bank and once the largest jewelry store in the country. It is truly a cornerstone of Salem history. To learn more or to request a private tour with our event planning team, call 978-745-5415 or email sales@colonialhallatrockafellas.com Weddings and events are offered at Colonial Hall year round

name: Crowninshield-Bentley House
blurb: The Crowninshield-Bentley House was built in 1727 for John Crowninshield, of the mercantile dynasty whose ships helped launch Salem's golden age. In the 1790s Reverend William Bentley moved in as a boarder — and Bentley is who you really want to know about. He was a Unitarian minister, a scholar of seventeen languages, a diarist who recorded Salem's daily life for three decades, and an abolitionist. His diaries — covering 1784 to 1819 — are one of the most important primary sources for early American history. He died in this house in 1819, exactly while Salem's maritime wealth was cresting. Th

name: Custom House
blurb:
The Salem Custom House stands as a testament to the city’s rich maritime history. Featured prominently in Nathaniel Hawthorne’s *The Scarlet Letter*, the building served as the office where he worked as Surveyor of the Port. Originally built to oversee trade and collect duties, the Custom House embodies Salem’s past as a vital hub of international commerce and continues to be a significant landmark today.

You're standing before the building that launched a literary career. The Custom House, built in 1819, wasn't just a place to collect tariffs on ships’ cargo; it was the setting for the openi

name: Daniels House Historic Site
blurb:
The Daniels House Historic Site preserves a rare example of a mid-18th century Georgian home in Salem. Built around 1750, the house remained in the Daniels family for over a century and showcases the lifestyle of a prosperous merchant family during the Revolutionary and early Federal periods. Today, the house is open to the public as a museum, offering guided tours and exhibits that bring Salem's colonial past to life.

The Daniels House, built around 1667, is one of the oldest inns in the United States. Originally a private home for Stephen Daniels, a mariner, it was expanded over the 17th an

name: Derby-Beebee Summer House
blurb: Built for a member of the Derby family, it was completed in 1796 and designed by the famous woodcarver and architect, Samuel McIntire.  Summer houses offered a quiet retreat during the hotter months and were often located in the most scenic spot to take in the best possible view.  They were often used to entertain guests for tea or light lunches, or simply to relax in private.  Inspired by English architecture, they were designed to showcase the owner’s wealth.  This was once on the farm of Lucius BeeBee in Wakefield but moved to the Peabody Essex grounds.  It is only one of two surviving summ

name: Derby House
blurb:
Built in 1762 as a wedding present, the Derby House was the home of Elias Hasket Derby (1739-1799) and Elizabeth Crowninshield Derby (1727-1799) for the first twenty years of their marriage.

This is the Derby House, built in 1762 — the oldest surviving brick house in Salem. Elias Hasket Derby, who lived here, made his fortune in the post-Revolution China trade and became one of the first millionaires in America. The house is small by later standards, but in 1762 it was a statement: brick was imported and expensive, and most Salem houses at the time were modest clapboard. Stand back and look —

name: Derby Powderhouse Wharf
blurb: None

name: Derby Wharf
blurb:
Begun in 1762 and completed in 1806, it extends 2,045 feet into the Salem Harbor. At one time, it was home to nearly twenty structures, During the Revolution privateers used the wharf probably more than any other port facility in the colonies.

Walk out onto Derby Wharf. Two thousand feet long, built starting in 1762 for Elias Hasket Derby, it was once the longest wharf in Salem — long enough for the deepest-drafted ships to unload at any tide. From this pier, Derby's ships sailed to every major port in the world: Canton, Calcutta, Saint Petersburg, Bordeaux, Havana, Mocha. Derby is often call

name: Derby Wharf Light Station
blurb:
The Derby Wharf Light Station, built in 1871, served a vital role in guiding ships safely into Salem Harbor during the city’s maritime heyday. Its unique square design sets it apart from traditional round lighthouses. Today, the light station is preserved as part of the Salem Maritime National Historic Site, offering visitors a glimpse into Salem’s rich seafaring past.

You're looking at a beacon that guided ships home for over a century. The Derby Wharf Light Station, built in 1874, stands at the end of this pier, marking the heart of Salem's maritime past. Before GPS, before radar, this smal

name: East Church
blurb: The East Church housed in the Gothic Revival built between 1844 and 1846 by the New York architect, Minard LaFever.  LaFever built a large number of churches in the early nineteenth century several of which are designated National Historic Landmarks including the St. Ann and Holy Trinity Church in Brooklyn.  Many of the choices for his construction are visible including pointed arched windows and the use of brownstone.  The front octagonal towers were higher at some point but cut down to their present height.  The East Church broke off from the First Church at the turn of the eighteenth centur

name: East India Fountain
blurb:
Known as Salem’s Gateway to the Far East, the fountain was completed in 1976 and designed by John Collins.  Collins studied at Pennsylvania State University and Harvard School of Design.  He was a landscape architect with a partner in the design firm of Collins DuTot and Associates.  

This fountain showcases his work as he had a background in landscape architecture and was interested in fusing natural history with cultural importance of a place.  Salem’s fountain shows the changing topography of the shoreline.  The bottom layer represents the current shoreline of Salem and the top layer that

name: Essex Institute Historic District
blurb:
The Essex Institute Historic District, located on Essex Street in Salem, comprises a group of historic buildings associated with the Essex Institute, founded in 1848 and merged into the Peabody Essex Museum in 1992. These buildings, including the Crowninshield-Bentley House, Gardner-Pingree House, and John Tucker Daland House, represent a significant concentration of Federal and Greek Revival architecture. The district was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1972.

You’re standing in the heart of what was once Salem’s intellectual life. This isn’t one building, but a collecti

name: First Church Meetinghouse 1692 Site
blurb:
Site of the First Church of Salem's 1692 meetinghouse where preliminary witch trial examinations were held. Now the Daniel Low Building.

You're standing where the First Church Meetinghouse stood in 1692 — the center of Puritan religious life in Salem Village. This is where accused witches were examined. This is where Martha Corey was excommunicated in September 1692, her husband Giles still refusing to plead in his own defense. This is where the community came to pray, to hear sermons, and then to accuse one another. The building is gone. What remains is the ground, and the memory that a cris

name: Fort Lee
blurb:
Fort Lee is a historic American Revolutionary War fort located on Salem Neck. Originally built in 1776 with a garrison of 100 artillerymen, it played a vital role in coastal defense. The fort was rebuilt and reinforced several times, seeing action in the War of 1812, the Civil War, and even the Spanish-American War. Today, visitors can explore the overgrown earthworks and a stone magazine, offering a glimpse into Salem’s military past.

Fort Lee is a remarkably well-preserved example of an American Revolutionary War fortification. Originally built in 1776 with a garrison of 100 artillerymen, i

name: Fort Pickering
blurb:
Fort Pickering, located on Winter Island, has served as a strategic defense for Salem Harbor since 1644. The present fort was constructed in 1863 by the Department of War during the Civil War, building upon earlier fortifications. Listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1973, it’s now maintained by the Town of Salem and offers a glimpse into centuries of coastal defense.

Look out toward the harbor. For nearly four centuries, someone has been watching from this spot. Fort Pickering, as you see it today, was built in 1863, but the story here began in 1643, with an earlier fort mea

name: Friends Burial Ground Quaker Cemetary
blurb: None

name: Friendship of Salem
blurb:
The Friendship of Salem is a full-scale replica of a three-masted frigate originally launched in 1797. The original vessel engaged in global trade, carrying goods like pepper, silk, and coffee, contributing to Salem’s prosperity. Lost at sea in 1812, the current Friendship was reconstructed in 2000 and now serves as a floating museum at Derby Wharf, operated by the National Park Service.

Look out at that ship. It isn't just a replica; it's a ghost made real. The *Friendship* you see before you represents a vessel launched in 1797, built right here in Salem by shipwright Enos Briggs. She wasn'

name: Gardner-Pingree House
blurb:
The Historic Gardner-Pingree House, located at 128 Essex Street, dates back to the 18th century and stands as a testament to Salem’s rich maritime history. Originally the home of sea captain John Gardner, and later owned by John Pingree, the house showcases the lifestyle of a successful merchant family during Salem’s peak as a trading port. Today, it is a well-preserved example of colonial architecture and a reminder of Salem's past.

Look up at those delicate carvings around the door and windows — that's the signature of Samuel McIntire, a Salem woodcarver who became one of the most important

name: Gedney House
blurb:
The Gedney House, located at 21 High Street, is a remarkably preserved example of 17th-century architecture in Salem. Constructed around 1665 by Eleazor Gedney, a member of one of Salem’s earliest families, the house offers a glimpse into the lives of the town’s original settlers. Notably, Eleazor was the brother of Bartholomew Gedney, who served as a judge during the infamous 1692 Salem witch trials. Today, the Gedney House is a historic site open to the public.

You’re standing before one of the oldest houses in Salem, built around 1665. Look closely at the timber frame – it’s a rare survivi

name: Greenlawn Cemetery
blurb: None

name: Hale Farm
blurb: None

name: Harmony Grove Cemetery
blurb: Harmony Grove Cemetery is a nonprofit cemetery and crematory organization devoted to maintaining its grounds in perpetuity. Now spanning nearly 57 acres, Harmony Grove remains a serene and historically rich resting place, honoring Salem’s past while serving future generations with care and dignity.

name: Hathaway House
blurb: Welcome to one of the oldest surviving wooden houses in the United States. Built around 1668 for the sea captain John Turner, this dark, weathered house was the inspiration for Nathaniel Hawthorne's 1851 novel The House of the Seven Gables. Hawthorne's cousin Susanna Ingersoll owned it when he was young, and he visited often. When you look at the building you're looking at almost three and a half centuries of Salem sailors, ship owners, and ghosts. The distinctive gables — seven of them — were added and removed over generations as fashions changed; by the time Hawthorne wrote the novel many ha

name: Hawkes House
blurb:
The Hawkes House, located on Derby Street, was designed in 1801 by Samuel McIntire, one of the most influential architects in early America. Commissioned by prominent Salem residents Elias Hasket and Elizabeth Derby, the three-story house was never occupied by its original owners. Today, it’s preserved as part of Salem National Historic Site and recognized on the National Register of Historic Places for its architectural significance.

You're looking at a house that was built for a family who never lived in it. The Hawkes House, completed in 1801, was commissioned by Elias Hasket and Elizabeth

name: Historic Beverly
blurb: Join Historic Beverly to explore Beverly's connection to the Salem witchcraft era at the historic 1694 home of Rev. John Hale, located in downtown Beverly near restaurants and shopping. Free parking. Special programming throughout October includes Witches and Bitches: The Badass Women of Beverly walking tour on October 9 and 19th; Tales and Ales spooky tours with gentile Brewing Company on October 10: Hale Whodunit? October 17th; Spooky Tales at Hale on October 24th, Creature Double Feature: Spooky Tales and Ancient Burial Ground Walking Tour on October 31st. Reservations required for all prog

name: Historic Central Wharf
blurb: Historic Central Wharf, located at 94 Wharf Street, played a vital role in Salem’s maritime history, dating back to the 1700s. Once a busy center for trade with the West Indies, China, and Europe, the wharf saw the arrival of goods like spices, silk, and rum. Today, it’s preserved as part of Salem Maritime National Historic Site, offering visitors a glimpse into Salem’s rich seafaring past.

name: Historic Derby Wharf
blurb:
Derby Wharf, now part of Salem Maritime National Historic Site, was once the heart of Salem's global trade network. Built in 1791, it quickly became the busiest wharf in the city, handling goods from around the world. Notably, the tea that triggered the Boston Tea Party first arrived in America at Derby Wharf in 1773. Today, visitors can walk the wharf and imagine the bustling activity of Salem's golden age of sail.

Derby Wharf, a National Historic Landmark, stands as a testament to Salem’s rich maritime past. Constructed in 1791 by Richard Derby, a prominent merchant, the wharf quickly becam

name: Historic New England’s Phillips House
blurb: Visit Historic New England's Phillips House on historic Chestnut Street and learn about the Phillips family and their domestic staff in the early 1900s. Antique carriages and cars. Admission charged.

name: Howard Street Cemetery
blurb: You're standing at the Howard Street Cemetery. Look toward the edge of the field — somewhere near here, on September 19, 1692, an 81-year-old farmer named Giles Corey was laid on his back in the dirt, a board placed across his chest, and stones piled on top. The court called it peine forte et dure — hard and forceful punishment. They did it because Corey refused to enter a plea. Plead guilty or innocent and the court seizes your estate; stay silent and your land passes to your sons. Corey stayed silent. He endured two days of stones. When the sheriff asked him if he would plead, he is said to

name: John Cabot House and Visitor Center
blurb: None

name: John Ward House
blurb: The John Ward House, located at 9 Brown Street, is a remarkable example of First Period architecture, dating back to between 1684 and 1723. It stands as one of the earliest colonial-era homes in the United States and holds the distinction of being among the first opened to the public as a museum. Today, the house is owned and operated by the Peabody Essex Museum, offering visitors a glimpse into 17th-century life in Salem.

name: Joshua Ward House
blurb:
The Joshua Ward House, completed in 1784, stands as one of the first brick residences in Salem. Its Federal-style architecture and interior detailing are the work of renowned Salem builder Samuel McIntire, and it boasts the oldest surviving staircase crafted by his hand. Notably, George Washington specifically requested lodging at this house during his 1789 visit to Salem, cementing its place in local history.

Look up at the brickwork. This house, built in 1784, is one of the first in Salem constructed entirely of brick. It’s a statement of wealth and permanence in a town built on the sea. Bu

name: Ledger
blurb:
Ledger is a progressive New England restaurant located in the former Salem Savings Bank, established around 1818. The restaurant elevates classic 19th-century dishes and cocktails with modern techniques, offering a unique dining experience. In 2022, Ledger’s Chef Daniel Gursha was nominated for the James Beard Foundation’s “Best Chef Northeast” award.

Ledger is a progressive New England restaurant located in the beautifully restored Salem Savings Bank building, established around 1818. Chef Daniel Gursha and owner Matt O'Neil elevate classic 19th-century dishes and cocktails with contemporary

name: Lye - Tapley Shoe Shop
blurb: The Lye-Tapley Shoe Shop is a typical shoe shop of New England consisting of a wooden building and called a “ten footer.”

name: McIntire Historic District
blurb:
One of America's finest Federal-era architecture districts. Named for architect Samuel McIntire. Grand mansions from Salem's maritime golden age.

Welcome to the McIntire Historic District — block after block of Federal-era mansions built in Salem's golden age, most designed or ornamented by the self-taught Salem woodcarver-turned-architect Samuel McIntire. Between 1780 and 1830, Salem's merchant princes — captains returning wealthy from the China trade — commissioned McIntire to build houses that would announce their fortunes to the world. The result is one of the finest concentrations of Fed

name: Monumental Cemetery
blurb: None

name: Narbonne House
blurb:
The Narbonne House, located at 71 Essex Street, dates back to approximately 1675, when it was built for local butcher Thomas Ives. It remained in the Andrews family for generations, becoming associated with Sarah Narbonne, who lived her entire life within its walls. Today, the house is preserved as a museum by the Salem Maritime National Historic Site and is listed on the National Register of Historic Places.

You're looking at one of the oldest houses in Salem, built around 1675. That's nearly a century before the Revolution. It began as the home and shop of Thomas Ives, a butcher, supplying

name: Nathaniel Bowditch House
blurb:
Nathaniel Bowditch was a self taught mathematician. He discovered over 8,000 errors in Englishman John Hamilton Moore’s, The Practical Navigator. In 1802 he published his own American Practical Navigator, which remains the world standard.

This was the home of Nathaniel Bowditch, whose 1802 book The New American Practical Navigator revolutionized sailing. Bowditch — a self-taught Salem native who left school at ten and apprenticed in a ship chandler's shop — taught himself calculus, Latin, and astronomy by lamplight. He went on to compute tables that let an ordinary sailor calculate longitude

name: Nathaniel Hawthorne's Birthplace
blurb:
Nathaniel Hawthorne's Birthplace, located at 27 Hardy Street, is the home where the famed author was born on July 4, 1804. This National Historic Landmark is part of The House of the Seven Gables Historic District and offers visitors a unique look at the early life and influences of one of America’s greatest writers. The house is open daily and is a must-see for fans of Hawthorne and American literature.

Nathaniel Hawthorne's Birthplace, located at 27 Hardy Street, is a preserved 18th-century home where the celebrated author of *The Scarlet Letter* and *The House of the Seven Gables* was born

name: Nathaniel Hawthorne Statue
blurb:
The Nathaniel Hawthorne Statue, sculpted by Bela Lyon Pratt and installed in 1925, honors Salem’s most famous author. The bronze statue depicts Hawthorne seated on a rock, a contemplative pose reflecting his introspective writing style. Located on Hawthorne Boulevard near the Hawthorne Hotel, it serves as a popular gathering place and a reminder of the author’s enduring legacy in his hometown.

This bronze figure looking thoughtful and slightly downcast is Nathaniel Hawthorne, Salem's most famous native son, cast in 1925 for the centennial of his birth. Hawthorne was born a few blocks from her

name: Old Salem Jail Site
blurb:
Site of the 1692 jail where accused witches were held. Multiple accused died in these terrible conditions before ever reaching trial.

You are near the site of the 1692 Salem jail. The building itself is long gone, but the terror it held is not. Accused witches — most of them women, many of them poor — were chained in a windowless cellar while they waited for a court that was determined to find them guilty. Several died here before they ever reached trial, unable to afford even the cost of the chains pinning them to the wall. Take a moment. This is the quietest stop on the trail, and the heavi

name: Old South Trask Cemetery
blurb: None

name: Old Town Hall
blurb:
Completed in 1816, Old Town Hall stands as a testament to Salem’s civic pride and its early history as a bustling port. This Federal-style building served as the center of Salem’s municipal government for generations and remains a spacious and iconic landmark. Today, it’s a popular event venue, hosting gatherings in the beautifully preserved Colonnade and Great Hall.

You’re standing in the heart of old Salem, looking at a building that has witnessed nearly two centuries of the town’s story. Old Town Hall, built in 1816, wasn't just a place for town meetings; it was the very center of civic li

name: Paul Revere's Bell
blurb: The bell at 2 New Liberty Street was cast for the Reverend William Bentley in 1801 for the East Church.  The surname Revere is often associated with the Patriot, Paul Revere, subject of Henry Wadsworth Longfellow’s poem, “The Midnight Ride of Paul Revere.” By trade, Paul Revere was a renowned silversmith who made all sorts of objects.  In 1792, he took on the challenge to cast his first bell when it was discovered that there was a crack in the one at the Second Church in Boston.   Successful, he continued to cast bells, becoming known for the clarity of tone they produced.  Together with his s

name: Peabody Essex Museum
blurb:
The Peabody Essex Museum (PEM) in Salem traces its origins to 1799, when sea captains and merchants formed the East India Marine Society to collect artifacts from their voyages abroad. Over time, the society’s collection grew, eventually merging with the Essex Institute to become the Peabody Essex Museum we know today. PEM is now celebrated as one of the oldest continuously operating museums in the United States, with a vast collection of over 1.3 million pieces, including a world-renowned holding of Asian art.

You’re standing before a museum that began as a passion for the far corners of the

name: Peabody Essex Museum
blurb:
The Ropes Mansion Garden, located at 318 Essex Street, is a beautifully preserved one-acre Colonial Revival garden created in 1912 by Salem’s own John Robinson. Situated within Salem’s McIntire Historic District, the garden welcomes visitors 365 days a year, offering a peaceful respite and a glimpse into early 20th-century garden design. A notable feature is the garden's 100-year-old copper beech tree.

You're looking at a garden that's a century old, but feels much older. This isn't a wilderness; it's a carefully constructed echo of Salem's colonial past. Designed in 1912 by local botanist Jo

name: Pedrick Store House
blurb:
Moved from Marblehead, originally built by Thomas Pedrick in 1770. During the Revolution, Pedrick commissioned privateers to capture British merchant vessels, and probably stored captured cargo in his store house.

This small gambrel-roofed building is the Pedrick Store House, built around 1770 — a rare surviving colonial warehouse from Salem's golden age of sail. Ships coming back from Canton or Calcutta would unload cargo directly onto wharves like Derby's, and warehouses like this one stored the goods before they were sold or shipped inland. Pepper, silks, porcelain, tea, sugar, rum — the c

name: Peirce-Nichols House
blurb:
The Peirce–Nichols House, located at 80 Federal Street, is a significant example of the work of Samuel McIntire, one of America’s first recognized master builders. Originally built as a residence, it showcases McIntire’s early style and has been preserved as a house museum. The Peabody Essex Museum now owns and operates the house, offering visitors a glimpse into Salem’s architectural heritage and the life of a prominent artisan.

Look up at the delicate carving above the doorway. You're standing before one of the earliest masterpieces of Samuel McIntire, Salem's most celebrated builder. He be

name: Pioneer Village
blurb:
Pioneer Village, established in 1930, holds the distinction of being the first living history museum in the United States. Built to commemorate the tercentennial of Massachusetts, it offers visitors a glimpse into 17th-century Salem with reconstructed colonial buildings, gardens, and demonstrations of early American life. Explore dugouts, thatched-roof cottages, and the Governor's Faire House to experience the world of the first settlers.

Pioneer Village, also known as Salem 1630, holds the distinction of being the first living history museum in the United States, opening in 1930 to commemora

name: Plummer Hall
blurb: At first glance this building seems rather imposing, but it is two buildings combined.  When John Tucker Daland first purchased the land in 1834, there was already a house in place.  After sixteen years, Daland moved the house by oxen to another part of town.  A successful merchant, he hired a Boston architect to design an Italianate-style mansion.  After the death of Daland in 1858, his widow lived there until her death where it passed through the family until acquired by the Essex Institute in August of 1885 for $5,600.

name: Prescott Memorial Cemetery
blurb: None

name: Proctor's Ledge Memorial
blurb:
Proctor's Ledge Memorial commemorates the events of 1692, specifically the first accusation that sparked the Salem Witch Trials. On February 25th, 1692, the afflicted girls first accused Tituba, Sarah Good, and Sarah Osborne of witchcraft after observing them near this ledge. Today, the memorial serves as a place for reflection on this dark chapter in Salem's history and a reminder of the dangers of mass hysteria and injustice.

Proctor's Ledge Memorial commemorates the events of 1692, specifically the first accusation that ignited the Salem Witch Trials. Located on a small wooded hill, the me

name: Public Stores
blurb: It was used by the U.S. Customs Service to hold cargo for merchants until they were able to pay the duties on their goods. Cargo came into Salem in barrels, crates, bags, and chests.

name: Punto Urban Art Museum
blurb: Located in the Point Neighborhood, Punto Urban Art Museum is a beautiful open-air museum that features 75 large-scale murals by 30 world-renowned artists and 25 local artists, all within a three-block radius. Only a five-minute walk from downtown and free to explore, this museum believes that public art can strengthen communities and help break down invisible socio-economic barriers. Special Exhibitions Punto Urban Art Museum also offers an assortment of special exhibitions that you should check out. A Dream Called Home - Inspired by award-winning, Mexican-American author Reyna Grande’s memoir

name: Quaker Meeting House
blurb:
The first Quakers, also known as the Society of Friends, arrived in New England in 1656.  The two women were immediately seized and deported.  Laws were passed to prevent further passage of the religious sect aboard ships, but the Quakers were intent on coming.  They faced intense persecution by the Congregationalists, the group we now refer to as Puritans, and were often fined, whipped, imprisoned, and even executed for their beliefs.  
A notable supporter of the Quakers in Salem was Thomas Maule who arrived from England in 1669.   He was to become a merchant who owned various plots of land.

name: Rebecca Nurse Homestead
blurb: Home of Rebecca Nurse, wrongfully executed in 1692. Includes original house, reproduction meetinghouse, and family cemetery in Danvers (original Salem Village).

name: Red’s Sandwich Shop
blurb:
Red's Sandwich Shop has been proudly serving Salem since 1945. Located in the historic London Coffee House – a building dating back to 1698 and once a meeting place for Patriots before the American Revolution – Red's offers hearty breakfasts, delicious sandwiches, and a taste of Salem's rich history. It's a local favorite and a must-visit for anyone seeking a true Salem experience.

Red's Sandwich Shop, established in 1945, is a Salem favorite known for its hearty breakfasts, lunches, and classic American fare. Uniquely, Red’s is located within the historic London Coffee House, a building dati

name: Roger Conant Statue
blurb:
Located at 2 Brown Street, the Roger Conant Statue honors a pivotal figure in Salem's history. Conant arrived with the first settlers in 1626 and served as their governor, guiding the fledgling colony through its initial hardships. He is considered a founder of both Salem and the Massachusetts Bay Colony, and this statue serves as a lasting tribute to his leadership and vision.

Located at 2 Brown Street, the Roger Conant Statue honors a pivotal figure in Salem’s history. Conant arrived in 1626 with the initial settlers of Naumkeag (later Salem) and served as their governor for several years.

name: Ropes Mansion
blurb:
The Ropes Mansion, located at 318 Essex Street, is a historic Georgian residence built between 1727 and 1729 and occupied by the Ropes family for nearly two centuries. Today, it operates as a museum, showcasing original furnishings and offering a glimpse into 18th and 19th-century life. Recognized as one of New England’s most significant historic houses, the Ropes Mansion offers a unique look into Salem’s past.

You're looking at one of the most complete Georgian homes in New England. The Ropes Mansion, begun in 1727, wasn't built for show – it was built to last. Four generations of the Ropes

name: Saint Marys Cemetery
blurb: None

name: Salem
blurb:
Salem, Massachusetts, established in 1626, boasts a rich and complex history reflected in a collection of maps held by the Library of Congress. These maps, including printed versions, photocopies, and facsimiles, document the city's development and may reveal details of landowners or tourism information. The collection focuses on maps not already cataloged separately, providing a unique resource for researchers and history enthusiasts.

Salem, Massachusetts, was founded in 1626 and has a rich history reflected in a collection of maps held by the Library of Congress. These maps, including print

name: Salem Athenæum
blurb:
The Salem Athenæum, located at 337 Essex Street, traces its origins back to 1760 with the founding of the Social Library and the Salem Philosophical Library. These two institutions merged in 1810 to form the Athenæum, creating a continuous tradition of learning and community engagement in Salem. Today, the Athenæum boasts a collection of over 50,000 volumes and hosts a variety of cultural programs, concerts, lectures, and performances.

The Salem Athenæum, located at 337 Essex Street, traces its origins back to 1760 with the founding of the Social Library and the Salem Philosophical Library. T

name: Salem Common
blurb:
Salem Common, a 7-acre park in the heart of the city, holds the distinction of being the oldest public park in the United States, established in 1637. Originally purchased as a training field for the militia and a pasture, it played a somber role during the 1692 witch trials as a site for public humiliation. By the 19th century, it was transformed into a Victorian-era park, and today it remains a vibrant public space for recreation and community events.

Salem Common, a 7-acre park in the heart of the city, holds the distinction of being the oldest public park in the United States, dating back

name: Salem Maritime National Historical Park
blurb:
Salem Maritime National Historical Park encompasses twelve historic structures and nine acres along Salem Harbor, interpreting the city’s pivotal role in maritime trade from the colonial period through the 19th century. Established as the first National Historic Site in 1938, the park tells the story of the Triangle Trade, Revolutionary War privateering, and the lucrative trade with the Far East. Managed by the National Park Service, it remains a vital link to Salem’s seafaring past.

You're standing at the heart of what made Salem famous long before the witch trials. Look around at these whar

name: Salem Maritime National Historic Park / Central Wharf
blurb: None

name: Salem Maritime National Historic Site
blurb:
Salem Maritime National Historic Site preserves a vital part of Salem’s history as a leading maritime center. Established in 1938 as the nation’s first National Historic Site, the park encompasses wharfs, buildings, and landscapes that tell the story of colonial trade, industrialization, and the complex history of slavery and freedom. Designated a National Historic Landmark in 1966 and listed on the National Register of Historic Places, the site offers a glimpse into Salem’s pivotal role in American commerce.

You're standing at the heart of what made Salem, Salem. Before the witch trials, bef

name: Salem Pioneer Village
blurb:
Salem Pioneer Village is a unique living-history museum offering visitors a glimpse into 17th-century colonial life. This recreated village features historically accurate buildings and costumed interpreters demonstrating daily life in early Salem. Open seasonally from June through September, it provides an immersive experience for all ages.

Salem Pioneer Village is a seasonal living-history museum dedicated to recreating a 17th-century colonial village. Located in Salem, Massachusetts, the village offers visitors an immersive experience of early colonial life through historically accurate bui

name: Salem Public Library
blurb:
The Salem Public Library, located at 370 Essex Street, occupies a striking brick mansion built in 1855 for Captain John Bertram, a prominent Salem merchant. Bertram rose from humble beginnings to become a successful ship owner and philanthropist. Today, the library serves as a vital community resource and is part of the North of Boston Library Exchange (NOBLE), providing access to a vast network of knowledge and services.

Look up at this building. It wasn't always a library. In 1855, this was the grand home of John Bertram, a Salem merchant who built his fortune on the sea. He started with al

name: Salem Willows
blurb:
Salem Willows is a historic oceanfront park in Salem, Massachusetts, originally planted in 1801 with European white willow trees. Intended as a tranquil shaded walk for patients at a nearby smallpox hospital, the area evolved into a popular public park by 1858. An amusement park was added in 1880, and today Salem Willows continues to offer visitors a classic seaside experience with its arcade, popcorn, and promenade.

Salem Willows, a scenic oceanfront park, has been a popular destination since the early 19th century. Originally planted with European white willow trees in 1801 to provide a tra

name: Salem Witch Trials Memorial
blurb:
Dedicated on November 2, 1992, the Salem Witch Trials Memorial stands as a poignant tribute to the victims of the 1692 witch trials. Designed by James Cutler and Maggie Smith, the memorial features twenty granite benches—one for each of the nineteen people hanged and one for Giles Corey, who was pressed to death. Located on the site of the former Salem Jail, it offers a space for quiet reflection on this tragic period in American history.

You’re standing in a place of profound sorrow and remembrance. The Salem Witch Trials Memorial, dedicated in 1992 on the 300th anniversary of the trials, is

name: Scale House
blurb:
used as a storage facility for scales and other equipment required to weigh cargo goods on ships. The scales were never used inside the building, but were carted and moved to the wharf and assembled alongside a vessel that had just returned.

The Scale House, built in 1829, is part of the Salem Maritime National Historic Site and was used by customs officials to weigh cargo coming off the ships. Incoming merchandise — pepper, tea, coffee, cocoa, silks — had duties levied by weight. A cheating scale could cost a captain a fortune or let a government lose one. The instruments inside were precisi

name: Ship Tavern Site
blurb:
Approximate site of the Ship Tavern, a key public gathering place in 1692 where trial rumors and accusations spread through the community.

The Ship Tavern stood near here in 1692 — one of the village's lesser-known but important gathering places. Taverns played a complicated role during the witch crisis: they were where rumors were traded, where accusations first formed, where the accused were sometimes mocked, and where the accusers gathered. Several examinations of accused witches happened near here. The Ship is long gone. What remains is the understanding that a tavern was as much a politi

name: St. Joseph Hall
blurb: By the 20th century, the western end of Derby Street had become the heart of the Polish Community in Salem. The St. Joseph Society, a fraternal society, built this headquarters in 1909.

name: The Burying Point
blurb: Known as the Burying Point, this historic cemetery on Charter Street dates back to 1637, making it Salem’s oldest burial ground. It serves as the final resting place for notable figures from Salem’s history, including several judges who presided over the infamous 1692 witch trials, as well as Revolutionary War veterans. The cemetery offers a somber yet fascinating glimpse into the lives and legacies of those who shaped Salem’s past.

name: The First Church Location
blurb: The land upon which the Witch Museum sits upon has ties to the Salem Witch Trials.  The Reverend John Higginson, a minister of the First Church, lived on the site.  His father organized the First Church, and John Higginson would go on to minister for 48 years.  His daughter, Ann Dolliver, was accused of witchcraft during the trials.  Abandoned by her husband and left with three children, Ann moved back into her father’s house in Salem from Gloucester.  In June of 1692, she was charged with having committed “sundry acts of witchcraft.” Her accusers were three of the afflicted girls who helped s

name: The First Muster
blurb: Stop here a moment. You're standing on the ground where the American citizen-soldier tradition began. On April 30, 1637, Salem mustered the first militia companies in the English North American colonies — not one regiment, but three: the North, South, and East. Every able-bodied man was required to report with his own musket, to drill, and to defend the town. That date — April 30, 1637 — is why the modern U.S. National Guard traces its founding to Salem Common. This small monument marks the occasion. Three hundred eighty-some years later, every time a Guardsman is deployed, the line runs back

name: The House of the Seven Gables
blurb:
The House of Seven Gables, a 1668 mansion, is famous as the inspiration for Nathaniel Hawthorne’s novel of the same name. Originally built by sea captain John Turner, the house remained in the Turner and Ingersoll families for generations. In 1910, Caroline Emmerton purchased the house and transformed it into a settlement house, preserving a vital piece of Salem’s past while serving the needs of the local immigrant community. Today, it operates as a museum dedicated to both Hawthorne’s legacy and Emmerton’s vision.

You’re looking at a house that’s more famous for what happened *inside* its wa

name: The Merchant
blurb:
Located at 148 Washington Street, The Merchant is a contemporary hotel housed within a beautifully restored 18th-century redbrick building. Offering luxurious guest rooms and high-end amenities, The Merchant provides a privately tucked away experience in the heart of Salem. The hotel is a popular choice for visitors seeking a comfortable and stylish base for exploring the city's historic attractions.

The Merchant is a boutique hotel located at 148 Washington Street in Salem, Massachusetts. Housed within a charming redbrick building with roots in the 1700s, it provides guests with luxurious ac

name: The Salem Inn
blurb:
The Salem Inn comprises three historic homes – the 1799 Hawthorne House, the 1834 West House, and the 1860 Charter Street House – all located in the heart of downtown Salem. Each house offers uniquely decorated rooms and suites, providing a cozy and intimate lodging experience. The Inn is known for its traditional New England charm and complimentary continental breakfast, making it a popular choice for visitors seeking a step back in time.

The Salem Inn comprises three historic homes on Summer Street, each with its own unique character and dating back to the early 1800s. These homes have been

name: The Witch House at Salem
blurb:
The Witch House, located at 310 Essex Street, is a remarkably preserved 17th-century home and the only structure in Salem with direct ties to the 1692 witch trials. It was the home of Judge Jonathan Corwin, one of the magistrates who presided over the trials. Today, visitors can tour the house and learn about 17th-century life and the events surrounding the infamous trials.

The Witch House at Salem, located at 310 Essex Street, is a remarkably preserved 17th-century home with a chilling connection to the infamous Salem Witch Trials. Built around 1650, it was the home of Judge Jonathan Corwin,

name: Washington Arch
blurb: Look up at Washington Arch. This gateway into Salem Common was erected in 1805 to honor George Washington, who had visited the town in 1789. Salem gave Washington a hero's welcome that day — he stayed at the Joshua Ward House just a few blocks from here. The arch you're looking at isn't the original; the 1805 wooden one rotted and was rebuilt in sturdier form. Walk through it and you're stepping onto a nine-acre green that has been the town's gathering place since the 1630s. Militia drilled here. Cattle grazed here. Every Fourth of July for more than two centuries, Salem has come here to celeb

name: Waterside Cemetery
blurb: None

name: West India Goods Store
blurb:
The term “West India Goods Store” was used in Salem as a generic term for a retail shop selling items from all over the world, not just the Caribbean. Captain Henry Prince (1764-1846) built this structure in the early 1800s.

The West India Goods Store, operated by the National Park Service inside Salem Maritime National Historic Site, recreates the kind of shop a 19th-century Salem household would have provisioned from. Ships coming home from the Caribbean and South America brought sugar, molasses, coffee, spices, tropical fruits, and rum; shops like this one retailed those goods to the publi

name: Wilson Cemetery
blurb: None

name: Winter Island Light
blurb:
Winter Island Light, also known as Fort Pickering Lighthouse, was established in 1871 to aid navigation into Salem Harbor, working in concert with other nearby lights.  Originally built to ensure safe passage for maritime traffic, it now functions as a popular campsite and recreational area, offering visitors a chance to explore its historic grounds and enjoy the coastal scenery. Guests are welcome to view the exterior of the lighthouse.

Look out toward the harbor. That light on Winter Island has been guiding ships safely into Salem since 1871. Before GPS, before radar, before even reliable c

name: Witch Trials Memorial
blurb: None

name: World War II Memorial
blurb: The World War II Memorial on Salem Common lists the names of Salem residents who gave their lives in the conflict. Walk its length. Some served in Europe, some in the Pacific, some in convoys across the North Atlantic. For a maritime city with deep Navy traditions, the Pacific names are particularly numerous. Each one was someone's son, brother, father. Each one walked this Common as a child, the way you're walking it now. Take a moment.

name: Yin Yu Tang House @Peabody Essex Museum
blurb:
Yin Yu Tang is a remarkable example of a traditional Qing Dynasty home, originally built in China around 1800. Carefully dismantled, shipped to Salem, and painstakingly reassembled, it now resides within the Peabody Essex Museum. Visitors can explore this authentic dwelling, offering a rare glimpse into the life of a prosperous merchant family in 19th-century China. It’s a unique cultural treasure and a testament to the Peabody Essex Museum’s global collections.

The Yin Yu Tang House inside the Peabody Essex Museum is a two-hundred-year-old Chinese merchant's house — the Huang family home fro

name: First Baptist Church of Salem
blurb: None

name: First Church in Salem
blurb: First Church in Salem traces its roots back to 1629, making it one of the oldest Protestant churches in North America. The current building, designed by architect Solomon Willard, was completed in 1836 and stands as a testament to Salem’s enduring religious heritage. Throughout its history, the church has been a beacon for progressive activism, with early members advocating for social justice and religious freedom.

name: First United Methodist Church
blurb: None

name: Grace Episcopal Church
blurb:
Grace Episcopal Church, located at 385 Essex Street in Salem, is a vibrant Episcopal congregation offering a full liturgical calendar. Services include Maundy Thursday with the Stripping of the Altar, Good Friday observances, the Great Vigil of Easter, and Choral Holy Eucharist on Easter Day. The church also fosters community through its regular newsletter, GraceNotes.

Grace Episcopal Church, located at 385 Essex Street, is a vibrant Episcopal congregation in Salem, Massachusetts. The church offers a full liturgical calendar, including special services for Maundy Thursday, Good Friday, and Ea

name: Hermetic Arts Learning Center
blurb:
Located at 96 Boston Street, the Hermetic Arts Learning Center is a bookstore, workshop space, and community hub dedicated to the study of Hermeticism, Western esoteric traditions, and magical practices. Open since 2014, it provides resources and education for those interested in exploring these often-overlooked areas of knowledge. The Center is open Monday, Thursday, Friday, Saturday, and Sunday from 11:00 AM to 6:00 PM.

The Hermetic Arts Learning Center, located at 96 Boston Street, is a bookstore, workshop space, and community center dedicated to the study of Hermeticism, magic, and esoter

name: Remix Church
blurb: None

name: Saint Vasilios Church
blurb: None

name: St. John The Baptist Ukrainian Church
St. Nicholas Orthodox Church
blurb:
St. Nicholas Orthodox Church has served Salem’s Orthodox Christian community since 1901, originally founded by immigrants from Galicia. The parish became part of the Russian Orthodox Church in 1908 and later joined the Orthodox Church in America in 1970. Today, St. Nicholas offers Divine Liturgy on Sunday mornings and welcomes visitors to experience a full and sensory worship service rooted in Holy Scripture and the traditions of the Early Church.

St. Nicholas Orthodox Church in Salem was established in 1901 by immigrants from Galicia, initially as part of the Russian Orthodox Church. In 1970

name: St. Peter’s-San Pedro Episcopal Church
blurb: None

name: Tabernacle Church
blurb: None

name: The Salvation Army North Shore Community Center & Church
The Satanic Temple
blurb:
The Satanic Temple, headquartered in Salem, Massachusetts, is a non-theistic organization that advocates for empathy, reason, and secularism. Founded on the principles of social justice, TST engages in activism and legal action, and is best known for its After School Satan program, offered as an alternative to Christian-based after-school clubs where those programs already exist.

The Satanic Temple, headquartered in Salem, Massachusetts, is a non-theistic organization established in 2013. It advocates for empathy, reason, and secularism, and engages in activism and legal action on issues such

name: Wesley United Methodist Church
blurb:
Wesley United Methodist Church’s roots in Salem stretch back to the early 19th century, when a small group began meeting for worship. The congregation first gathered in a storefront before acquiring a building on Harbor Street, and in 1888 dedicated its current building at 8 North Street. Today, Wesley UMC continues to offer Sunday services, welcoming visitors with traditional hymns, contemporary music, and a focus on spiritual growth.

Wesley United Methodist Church’s roots in Salem stretch back to the early 19th century, when a small group of Methodists began meeting for worship. The congreg
